mission.title: Install and Configure Spiderpool on Kubernetes
mission.description: Spiderpool is an underlay and RDMA network solution for Kubernetes, designed to enhance networking capabilities for bare metal, virtual machines, and public cloud environments. Installing Spiderpool allows you to leverage its high-performance networking features, particularly beneficial for network I/O-intensive applications.

steps.title: Add the Spiderpool Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Spiderpool Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add spiderpool https://spidernet-io.github.io/spiderpool
```

steps.title: Update Helm repositories
steps.description:
Update your local Helm chart repository cache to ensure you have the latest version of the Spiderpool chart:
```bash
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Spiderpool using Helm
steps.description:
Install Spiderpool in the `kube-system` namespace with the specified version:
```bash
helm install spiderpool spiderpool/spiderpool --version 1.1.1 --namespace kube-system --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Spiderpool pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep spiderpool
```

steps.title: Verify CRDs
steps.description:
Verify that the Spiderpool Custom Resource Definitions were created:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep spiderpool.spidernet.io
```
You should see spiderippools, spidersubnets, spiderendpoints, spidercoordinators, spidermultusconfigs, and spiderreservedips.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Spiderpool will show the Spiderpool pods running in the `kube-system` namespace. You can further customize the configuration as needed to suit your network requirements.
codeSnippets: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep spiderpool

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Spiderpool Helm release from the kube-system namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall spiderpool --namespace kube-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs
uninstall.description:
Remove all Custom Resource Definitions associated with Spiderpool:
```bash
kubectl get crd -o name | grep spiderpool.spidernet.io | xargs kubectl delete
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of Spiderpool. You can use the following command to export the current ConfigMap:
```bash
kubectl get configmap spiderpool-conf -n kube-system -o yaml > spiderpool-conf-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update your Helm repository and upgrade the Spiderpool release:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade spiderpool spiderpool/spiderpool --namespace kube-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Spiderpool pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep spiderpool
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Spiderpool pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system
```
Look for any configuration issues or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Network connectivity issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you experience network connectivity issues, verify the Spiderpool configuration:
```bash
kubectl get configmap spiderpool-conf -n kube-system -o yaml
```
Ensure that the settings are correct and that the necessary network policies are applied.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify the release name and namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace kube-system
```
Ensure that 'spiderpool' is listed in the output.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources for pods
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are not scheduling due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n kube-system
```
Adjust the resource requests in the configuration if necessary.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed.
